verses.text: And the sons of the prophets said unto Elisha, Behold now, the place where we dwell with thee is too strait for us. Let us go, we pray thee, unto Jordan, and take thence every man a beam, and let us make us a place there, where we may dwell. And he answered, Go ye. Guidance: The prophetic community needs more space. The 'sons of the prophets' (benei ha-nevi'im) approach their master with a practical concern. 'The place where we dwell with thee is too strait for us.' Hebrew tzar mimmenu — 'is narrow for us.' The school has outgrown its meeting space. The detail is significant: the prophetic community is growing. Even during the apostasy of the northern kingdom, the LORD has been raising up a faithful prophetic remnant. Their proposal: 'Let us go unto Jordan, and take thence every man a beam, and let us make us a place there.' They will collectively work — each contributing a beam from the riverside trees — to build a larger dwelling near the Jordan. The plan is practical and communal. Elisha's reply: 'Go ye.' Hebrew lekhu — 'go.' He approves the project. The chapter is showing us the everyday life of the prophetic community. Between the dramatic confrontations with kings and pagan armies, the schools needed buildings, members did manual work, prophets gave routine approvals. The miraculous and the mundane coexist in Elisha's ministry. The chapter is teaching us a leadership principle. When subordinates bring sensible requests they can execute, the leader's job is to authorize and bless, not to micromanage. Elisha's two-word answer is itself a model of trust.

'The place where we dwell with thee is too strait for us.' Hebrew tzar mimmenu — 'is narrow for us.' The school has outgrown its meeting space. The detail is significant: the prophetic community is growing. Even during the apostasy of the northern kingdom, the LORD has been raising up a faithful prophetic remnant. Their proposal: 'Let us go unto Jordan, and take thence every man a beam, and let us make us a place there.' And one said, Be content, I pray thee, and go with thy servants. And he answered, I will go. So he went with them. And when they came to Jordan, they cut down wood. But as one was felling a beam, the axe head fell into the water: and he cried, and said, Alas, master! for it was borrowed. Guidance: The borrowed axe lost. One of the prophets-in-training presses: 'Be content, I pray thee, and go with thy servants.' Elisha agrees. They arrive at the Jordan and begin cutting trees. Then disaster: as one of the men is felling a beam, the iron axe-head separates from its wooden haft and flies off into the river. Iron in ninth-century Israel was a high-value commodity — owning an iron axe was beyond this poor student's means; he had borrowed one to participate in the building project. Now the axe is lost; he has incurred a debt he cannot pay. The young prophet's cry is heartrending: 'Alas, master! for it was borrowed.' Hebrew ahah adoni vehu sha'ul. The chapter is showing us how the LORD's miracles often address very humble concrete needs. This is not a national crisis. This is one poor student losing one borrowed tool in a river. The same prophetic ministry that confronts kings and heals lepers will now stoop to recover a piece of borrowed iron from a muddy river. The chapter is teaching us that the LORD cares about the small troubles of His servants. Elisha could have shrugged — 'the axe is gone; deal with it.' He does something else. The borrowed tool was important to the student; therefore it is important to the prophet.

Iron in ninth-century Israel was a high-value commodity — owning an iron axe was beyond this poor student's means; he had borrowed one to participate in the building project. And the man of God said, Where fell it? And he shewed him the place. And he cut down a stick, and cast it in thither; and the iron did swim. Therefore said he, Take it up to thee. And he put out his hand, and took it. Guidance: The iron swims. Elisha's question is practical: 'Where fell it?' The student points to the spot. Elisha cuts a stick and casts it into the water at the indicated spot. 'And the iron did swim.' Hebrew vayyatzef ha-barzel — 'and the iron floated.' Iron, being approximately seven times the density of water, does not float; the chapter is recording a miracle. The piece of wood thrown into the river at the place where the axe-head sank somehow caused the iron to rise to the surface. The chapter does not explain mechanism; the narrative is content with: the prophet threw a stick; the iron floated. Elisha's command: 'Take it up to thee.' The student reaches out and recovers the axe-head. The chapter is teaching us several things. (1) The LORD's miracles often work through the most ordinary materials. A stick — the cheapest, most available object — becomes the instrument of the miracle. The LORD does not require precious or rare elements; ordinary wood will do. (2) The student must still cooperate. Elisha does not pluck the iron from the water and hand it back; he tells the student, 'Take it up to thee.' The miracle creates the possibility; the recipient must reach out and receive it. (3) The reversal of natural law in service of small need is a signature of how the LORD relates to His people. He suspends physics to recover a borrowed tool. No grand demonstration; no national impact; just a tool returned, a debt avoided, a student spared anxiety. The chapter is showing us the largeness of the LORD's care expressed in the smallness of the occasion.

Then the king of Syria warred against Israel, and took counsel with his servants, saying, In such and such a place shall be my camp. And the man of God sent unto the king of Israel, saying, Beware that thou pass not such a place; for thither the Syrians are come down. And the king of Israel sent to the place which the man of God told him and warned him of, and saved himself there, not once nor twice. Guidance: The intelligence the prophet provides. The chapter jumps to a renewed Aramean military campaign. The Aramean king (Ben-hadad II) plans tactical movements with his war-council: 'In such and such a place shall be my camp.' He chooses concealed positions where he hopes to ambush Israelite forces or to mount surprise raids. But every time he sets a position, Elisha sends word to Jehoram of Israel: 'Beware that thou pass not such a place; for thither the Syrians are come down.' The prophet has prophetic intelligence — he knows exactly where the Aramean ambushes are being set. He passes the information to the Israelite king. The narrator's repetition: 'not once nor twice.' Hebrew lo achat ve-lo shtayim — 'not one [time] and not two.' This happens repeatedly. The campaign is being systematically frustrated by prophetic intelligence. The chapter is showing us the LORD's prophet as an instrument of military defense. Elisha is not on Jehoram's payroll; he holds no rank in the Israelite army; yet he provides intelligence that no human spy could provide. The LORD reveals enemy plans to His prophet, who passes them to His covenant king. The chapter is teaching us that the LORD's care for His covenant people extends to specific military tactical situations. The northern kingdom is in apostasy, but the LORD has not abandoned it; He still protects it through His prophet's word. The chapter is also setting up a confrontation. Ben-hadad will discover the leak; he will move to neutralize the leaker.

Therefore the heart of the king of Syria was sore troubled for this thing; and he called his servants, and said unto them, Will ye not shew me which of us is for the king of Israel? And one of his servants said, None, my lord, O king: but Elisha, the prophet that is in Israel, telleth the king of Israel the words that thou speakest in thy bedchamber. Guidance: The leak identified. Ben-hadad is enraged: 'The heart of the king of Syria was sore troubled.' Hebrew vayyissa'er lev — 'his heart raged.' He assumes there must be a traitor in his inner circle — someone is passing his plans to Israel. He calls his officers: 'Will ye not shew me which of us is for the king of Israel?' One of his servants corrects him: 'None, my lord, O king: but Elisha, the prophet that is in Israel, telleth the king of Israel the words that thou speakest in thy bedchamber.' The servant identifies the actual source of the leak. It is not a human traitor; it is a prophet across the border. Elisha tells Jehoram 'the words that thou speakest in thy bedchamber' — Hebrew chadar mishkavekha — 'the chamber of your lying-down.' Even Ben-hadad's most private conversations, conducted in his most secured private space, are known to Elisha. The chapter is showing us the LORD's prophetic perception at its widest reach. Distance does not block prophetic sight; royal security does not protect royal secrets; the LORD's prophet hears words spoken behind locked doors in foreign palaces. The Naaman story (chapter 5) has presumably circulated; the Aramean court knows about Elisha. The chapter is teaching us a profound theological truth. Privacy is a human construct that does not bind the LORD. The most secret conversations are open to the LORD's hearing; what He hears, He may relay to His prophet. The chapter is also showing us why pagan kings dread Israel's prophets in a way Israel's own apostate kings do not. Ben-hadad immediately recognizes that an Israelite prophet is the threat; Jehoram could barely think of Elisha as a resource. The pagan king has clearer theological perception of the prophet's power than the covenant king does.

And he said, Go and spy where he is, that I may send and fetch him. And it was told him, saying, Behold, he is in Dothan. Therefore sent he thither horses, and chariots, and a great host: and they came by night, and compassed the city about. Guidance: The Aramean cordon at Dothan. Ben-hadad's response is direct: 'Go and spy where he is, that I may send and fetch him.' He will physically capture the prophet — eliminating the intelligence-leak by eliminating the leaker. Aramean intelligence reports back: 'Behold, he is in Dothan.' Dothan is a small town about 11 miles north of Samaria, on the route between the Jezreel valley and the central hill country. The site has been identified archaeologically (Tell Dothan); it was a modest walled town in the Iron Age. Ben-hadad sends a full military expedition: 'horses, and chariots, and a great host' (Hebrew sus ve-rekhev ve-chayil kaved — 'horse and chariot and a heavy army'). An entire field force to capture one prophet. They arrive at night and 'compassed the city about' — surround Dothan, encircle the town, cut off escape. The chapter is showing us the disproportion of pagan response to prophetic threat. Ben-hadad sends an army for a man without a sword. The disproportion reveals how seriously he takes the threat — and also, in another sense, how foolish he is. An army can capture a man; it cannot capture the One who reveals to him. The chapter is teaching us that those who oppose the LORD often fight with the wrong weapons. Ben-hadad fields cavalry and chariots; the actual battle is being fought in a different domain entirely.

And when the servant of the man of God was risen early, and gone forth, behold, an host compassed the city both with horses and chariots. And his servant said unto him, Alas, my master! how shall we do? And he answered, Fear not: for they that be with us are more than they that be with them. And Elisha prayed, and said, LORD, I pray thee, open his eyes, that he may see. And the LORD opened the eyes of the young man; and he saw: and, behold, the mountain was full of horses and chariots of fire round about Elisha. Guidance: The unseen army revealed. Elisha's servant rises early — perhaps for his morning chores — and looks out. What he sees is terrifying: an Aramean army encircling the city. His cry: 'Alas, my master! how shall we do?' Hebrew ahah adoni eikhah na'aseh — 'Alas, my lord, how shall we act?' The situation looks hopeless. Elisha's reply is one of the great faith-statements of the OT: 'Fear not: for they that be with us are more than they that be with them.' Hebrew rabbim asher ittanu me-asher otam — 'many [are those] with us [more] than [those] with them.' Numerically impossible — by the visible count, the Aramean army vastly outnumbers Elisha and his servant. But Elisha sees beyond the visible. Elisha then prays: 'LORD, I pray thee, open his eyes, that he may see.' Hebrew YHWH peqach-na et-einav ve-yir'eh — 'LORD, open please his eyes that he may see.' The prayer is for the servant — not that the LORD do something new, but that the servant be enabled to see what is already there. 'The LORD opened the eyes of the young man; and he saw: and, behold, the mountain was full of horses and chariots of fire round about Elisha.' Hebrew vehineh ha-har male susim ve-rekhev esh — 'and behold the mountain full of horses and chariots of fire.' The Aramean cavalry is surrounded in turn by the LORD's fiery cavalry. The chariots of fire that took Elijah up (2 Kgs 2:11) are now visible, surrounding His new prophet. The chapter is showing us one of the foundational truths of biblical theology: the invisible spiritual realities are more substantial than the visible material realities. Behind every earthly army, the LORD has armies; behind every threat to His people, the LORD has His own forces. The servant could not see this; Elisha could. The prayer is not for new help to be sent but for the eyes to see what is already present. The chapter is teaching us a profoundly hopeful principle: faith is often a matter of seeing what is invisible to those without faith. The same situation viewed by the unfaith-eye is desperate; viewed by the faith-eye, it is full of divine resources. The chapter is also one of the great proof-texts for the doctrine of angelic protection of God's people. Elisha is not relying on diplomacy or military trick; he is relying on the LORD's heavenly army that visibly surrounds his enemies.

The chapter is showing us one of the foundational truths of biblical theology: the invisible spiritual realities are more substantial than the visible material realities. Behind every earthly army, the LORD has armies; behind every threat to His people, the LORD has His own forces. The servant could not see this; Elisha could. The prayer is not for new help to be sent but for the eyes to see what is already present. The chapter is teaching us a profoundly hopeful principle: faith is often a matter of seeing what is invisible to those without faith. The same situation viewed by the unfaith-eye is desperate; viewed by the faith-eye, it is full of divine resources. The chapter is also one of the great proof-texts for the doctrine of angelic protection of God's people. Elisha is not relying on diplomacy or military trick; he is relying on the LORD's heavenly army that visibly surrounds his enemies.

And when they came down to him, Elisha prayed unto the LORD, and said, Smite this people, I pray thee, with blindness. And he smote them with blindness according to the word of Elisha. And Elisha said unto them, This is not the way, neither is this the city: follow me, and I will bring you to the man whom ye seek. But he led them to Samaria. And it came to pass, when they were come into Samaria, that Elisha said, LORD, open the eyes of these men, that they may see. And the LORD opened their eyes, and they saw; and, behold, they were in the midst of Samaria. Guidance: The blinded army led to Samaria. The Aramean force advances on Elisha. He prays: 'Smite this people, I pray thee, with blindness.' Hebrew haqei-na et-ha-goi ha-zeh ba-sanverim — 'strike now this nation with sudden-blindness.' Hebrew sanverim is a specific word for a peculiar kind of disorientation/blindness; it appears in Gen 19:11 where the Sodomites are struck at Lot's door. It may be more a perceptual confusion than full visual blindness — a state in which seeing happens but recognition does not. 'He smote them with blindness according to the word of Elisha.' The Aramean troops can no longer recognize where they are or who is in front of them. Elisha speaks to them: 'This is not the way, neither is this the city: follow me, and I will bring you to the man whom ye seek.' The statement is technically true in a layered way (Dothan is not 'the way' to where he is about to lead them; he will indeed bring them to himself, though they think they are being led to someone else). He leads them on a 10-mile march south to Samaria — the capital, the Israelite king's seat. When they arrive in the middle of the city, Elisha prays again: 'LORD, open the eyes of these men, that they may see.' The blindness is lifted. They look around and discover they are in the middle of Samaria — the enemy capital — surrounded by Israelite forces, with no Aramean reinforcement anywhere. The chapter is showing us a brilliant prophetic operation. The same LORD's army that could have destroyed the Arameans outside Dothan instead funnels them into Israelite custody — alive, harmless, and in maximum diplomatic position. The chapter is teaching us that the LORD's victories often take forms more sophisticated than mere annihilation. He could have killed the Aramean army; He chose to neutralize and embarrass them. The capture-without-bloodshed will produce a result far more useful than a battlefield victory.

And the king of Israel said unto Elisha, when he saw them, My father, shall I smite them? shall I smite them? And he answered, Thou shalt not smite them: wouldest thou smite those whom thou hast taken captive with thy sword and with thy bow? set bread and water before them, that they may eat and drink, and go to their master. And he prepared great provision for them: and when they had eaten and drunk, he sent them away, and they went to their master. So the bands of Syria came no more into the land of Israel. Guidance: Mercy instead of slaughter. Jehoram, finding an entire Aramean platoon delivered into his hands, is eager to execute them. 'My father, shall I smite them? shall I smite them?' The repetition shows excitement — he wants quick prophetic approval to slaughter the captives. Note: 'my father' — avi — is the respectful term Jehoram now uses of Elisha; the king's relationship with the prophet has warmed since chapter 3, possibly because of all the recent intelligence Elisha has been providing. Elisha refuses: 'Thou shalt not smite them.' His reasoning is theologically sharp: 'Wouldest thou smite those whom thou hast taken captive with thy sword and with thy bow?' Jehoram did not capture these men with his own sword and bow — the LORD captured them. They are not Jehoram's prisoners; they are the LORD's. The proper treatment of them must follow the LORD's logic, not the king's. Elisha's prescription: 'Set bread and water before them, that they may eat and drink, and go to their master.' Feed them and send them home. Jehoram complies — and in fact does more than the minimum: 'He prepared great provision for them.' A feast, not just bread-and-water. The Arameans are fed lavishly and released. The diplomatic effect: 'So the bands of Syria came no more into the land of Israel.' The Aramean raids cease — at least for a season. The chapter is teaching us multiple foundational principles. (1) Mercy is sometimes more strategically effective than execution. (2) Prisoners taken by divine intervention belong to a different category than prisoners taken by force. (3) The covenant king who defers to the prophet's judgment finds the better outcome. The chapter is also paralleling Romans 12:20 (echoing Prov 25:21-22): 'If your enemy hungers, feed him.' The Elisha-Aramean sequence is the OT instantiation of this principle — kindness to the captured enemy as superior strategy and theology to vengeance.

And it came to pass after this, that Benhadad king of Syria gathered all his host, and went up, and besieged Samaria. And there was a great famine in Samaria: and, behold, they besieged it, until an ass's head was sold for fourscore pieces of silver, and the fourth part of a cab of dove's dung for five pieces of silver. Guidance: The siege and the famine. The chapter shifts forward in time. The peace from the previous episode has ended. 'Ben-hadad king of Syria gathered all his host, and went up, and besieged Samaria.' This is no raid; this is a full-scale siege of the Israelite capital. The siege's effect: 'There was a great famine in Samaria.' Hebrew ra'av gadol — extreme famine. The chapter quantifies the famine with two horrifying market notes. (1) 'An ass's head was sold for fourscore pieces of silver.' A donkey's head — the least edible part of a non-kosher animal — was selling for 80 shekels of silver. The donkey itself was unclean per Mosaic law (Lev 11:3); to eat one was a violation of food laws. The head was the worst portion. 80 shekels of silver was the cost of a slave; here it was the price of an animal organ. (2) 'The fourth part of a cab of dove's dung for five pieces of silver.' A 'cab' was a small measure of grain (about 2 quarts); a quarter-cab was roughly a half-quart. 'Dove's dung' is generally understood as a particular kind of carob pod or some other coarse fodder; some commentators take it literally as actual dove droppings used as a famine-survival food. Either way, a tiny measure of starvation-fodder was selling for 5 shekels of silver. The chapter is establishing the dire stage. Samaria is in collapse. The food shortage has produced market prices that reveal extreme desperation. The chapter is teaching us about the cost of national apostasy at full bloom. The Omride dynasty's accumulated sins are now producing the covenant curses Deuteronomy 28 had warned about — including specifically the siege-famine. The northern kingdom is reaping what it sowed.

And as the king of Israel was passing by upon the wall, there cried a woman unto him, saying, Help, my lord, O king. And he said, If the LORD do not help thee, whence shall I help thee? out of the barnfloor, or out of the winepress? And the king said unto her, What aileth thee? And she answered, This woman said unto me, Give thy son, that we may eat him to day, and we will eat my son to morrow. So we boiled my son, and did eat him: and I said unto her on the next day, Give thy son, that we may eat him: and she hath hid her son. Guidance: The cannibalism case. The chapter brings us to its most appalling scene. Jehoram is walking on the wall of Samaria — likely inspecting defenses or surveying the besieged populace. A woman cries up to him: 'Help, my lord, O king.' The king's exhausted response reveals his condition: 'If the LORD do not help thee, whence shall I help thee? out of the barnfloor, or out of the winepress?' Threshing floor and winepress are empty; the granaries are empty; the wine is gone. The king has nothing to give. The remark is partly true theology — the LORD alone can save in such situations — and partly bitter exhaustion. Then he asks what she wants: 'What aileth thee?' Her story is unspeakable. 'This woman said unto me, Give thy son, that we may eat him to day, and we will eat my son to morrow.' Two women, both with sons, both starving, had made a horrifying pact: cook one boy today, cook the other tomorrow. 'So we boiled my son, and did eat him.' She cooked her own son; both women ate the meat. 'And I said unto her on the next day, Give thy son, that we may eat him: and she hath hid her son.' The second woman defaulted on the pact. The first woman appeals to the king for justice — she wants the second mother's son produced for the agreed-upon second meal. The chapter is showing us covenant curses operative at their most extreme. Deut 28:53-57 had specifically warned that siege-famine would drive Israelites to eat their own children. The warning has come true in Samaria. The mother who can think of nothing but enforcing her cannibalistic contract is not a moral outlier; she is the inevitable product of national apostasy + sustained famine. The chapter is teaching us that the LORD's covenant curses are real, not rhetorical. The Deuteronomic warnings about siege-cannibalism were not symbolic; they described what actually happens when covenant people abandon their covenant. The chapter is also confronting the reader with the moral structure that has been violated. The woman's appeal to the king for the second son shows that even cannibalism has not extinguished her sense of contract-fairness. She is willing to eat children but expects the other mother to honor the agreement. The moral disintegration is total — even the residual moral framework she retains has been bent into the service of grotesquery.

And it came to pass, when the king heard the words of the woman, that he rent his clothes; and he passed by upon the wall, and the people looked, and, behold, he had sackcloth within upon his flesh. Then he said, God do so and more also to me, if the head of Elisha the son of Shaphat shall stand on him this day. Guidance: The king's torn clothes and oath against Elisha. Jehoram hears the cannibalism story and tears his clothes — the gesture of profound distress. 'He passed by upon the wall, and the people looked, and, behold, he had sackcloth within upon his flesh.' The torn outer garment reveals sackcloth (saq) underneath — the rough hairy cloth of mourning and repentance. Jehoram had been wearing sackcloth secretly under his royal robes; the tearing has exposed it. The chapter is showing us a complex king. He is in personal distress; he had been doing private mourning; the cannibalism revelation pushes the distress over the edge. Yet his next act is theological folly. 'God do so and more also to me, if the head of Elisha the son of Shaphat shall stand on him this day.' Hebrew koh ya'aseh-li elohim ve-khoh yosif im-ya'amod rosh elisha ben-shafat alav ha-yom. A formal oath that he will execute Elisha by sundown. The king blames the prophet for the famine — perhaps because the prophet had not intervened to break the siege; perhaps because the king had asked and Elisha had not provided. The logic: if there is famine, and the LORD's prophet is not lifting it, then the prophet is the problem. The chapter is teaching us about the bankruptcy of apostate theological reasoning. Jehoram has tracked the covenant curse to the prophet rather than to its actual source (his own and his dynasty's apostasy). The siege and famine are the LORD's discipline on Israel for forsaking covenant; eliminating the prophet does nothing to address the actual cause. The king's oath against Elisha is the act of a man blaming the messenger for the message.

And it came to pass, when the king heard the words of the woman, that he rent his clothes; and he passed by upon the wall, and the people looked, and, behold, he had sackcloth within upon his flesh. Then he said, God do so and more also to me, if the head of Elisha the son of Shaphat shall stand on him this day. Guidance: The king's torn clothes and oath against Elisha. Jehoram hears the cannibalism story and tears his clothes — the gesture of profound distress. 'He passed by upon the wall, and the people looked, and, behold, he had sackcloth within upon his flesh.' The torn outer garment reveals sackcloth (saq) underneath — the rough hairy cloth of mourning and repentance. Jehoram had been wearing sackcloth secretly under his royal robes; the tearing has exposed it. The chapter is showing us a complex king. He is in personal distress; he had been doing private mourning; the cannibalism revelation pushes the distress over the edge. Yet his next act is theological folly. 'God do so and more also to me, if the head of Elisha the son of Shaphat shall stand on him this day.' Hebrew koh ya'aseh-li elohim ve-khoh yosif im-ya'amod rosh elisha ben-shafat alav ha-yom. A formal oath that he will execute Elisha by sundown. The king blames the prophet for the famine — perhaps because the prophet had not intervened to break the siege; perhaps because the king had asked and Elisha had not provided. The logic: if there is famine, and the LORD's prophet is not lifting it, then the prophet is the problem. The chapter is teaching us about the bankruptcy of apostate theological reasoning. Jehoram has tracked the covenant curse to the prophet rather than to its actual source (his own and his dynasty's apostasy). The siege and famine are the LORD's discipline on Israel for forsaking covenant; eliminating the prophet does nothing to address the actual cause. The king's oath against Elisha is the act of a man blaming the messenger for the message.

But Elisha sat in his house, and the elders sat with him; and the king sent a man from before him: but ere the messenger came to him, he said to the elders, See ye how this son of a murderer hath sent to take away mine head? look, when the messenger cometh, shut the door, and hold him fast at the door: is not the sound of his master's feet behind him? And while he yet talked with them, behold, the messenger came down unto him: and he said, Behold, this evil is of the LORD; what should I wait for the LORD any longer? Guidance: Elisha forewarned. Elisha is at home, in conference with the elders of the city. The narrator interrupts the cannibalism scene's intensity with this calm domestic snapshot: the prophet at home, civic leaders gathered around him. The contrast with the king's wall-walking panic is deliberate. The king dispatches an executioner. Before the messenger arrives, Elisha speaks to the elders: 'See ye how this son of a murderer hath sent to take away mine head?' 'Son of a murderer' (Hebrew ben-ham'rate'ach) — a withering epithet. Jehoram is Ahab's son; Ahab had Naboth murdered (1 Kgs 21); the son of the man who arranged Naboth's death is now arranging Elisha's. The hereditary description is biting. Elisha's instruction to the elders: 'When the messenger cometh, shut the door, and hold him fast at the door: is not the sound of his master's feet behind him?' Two pieces of information. (1) Block the messenger at the door. (2) The king himself is coming behind the messenger; Elisha can hear the royal footsteps approaching. The chapter is showing us the prophet's full prophetic perception. He knows the executioner has been dispatched; he knows the king is coming behind to oversee or to reconsider; he positions the elders as a buffer. The chapter is also showing us his calm. He is not fleeing; he is not arming himself; he is sitting with the elders, planning the reception of his would-be killer's herald. As Elisha speaks, the messenger arrives. The messenger (or, more commonly read, the king himself arriving behind the messenger) declares: 'Behold, this evil is of the LORD; what should I wait for the LORD any longer?' Hebrew hineh-zot ha-ra'ah me'et YHWH mah-ochil la-YHWH od. The statement is the king's theological collapse. He acknowledges that the famine is from the LORD (correctly diagnostic). He concludes that since the LORD is the source of the evil, there is no point in waiting on the LORD for relief. He has given up; he will execute the prophet because he sees no other option. The chapter is teaching us the desperate condition of a covenant king who can correctly identify the LORD as the source of his troubles but cannot draw the right conclusion from that identification. The right conclusion would be: if the LORD is the source, then repent and seek the LORD. The king's conclusion is: if the LORD is the source, then there is no hope. He has not yet learned that the same LORD who sends the discipline also brings the relief. The chapter ends with the king at Elisha's door, the executioner held, the prophet alive, the king blaspheming, and an unresolved situation — which chapter 7 will dramatically resolve with the LORD's overnight lifting of the siege.
